column5: Replace discipline lectures with explicit teaching of SEL skills using modeling and role-play; incorporate a daily 5‑minute SEL routine (e.g., morning meeting) to practice specific social skills; co-plan SEL integration with a colleague and observe their implementation.
column6: Provide the teacher with resources on CASEL SEL competencies and model a morning meeting. Observe a lesson, then debrief using student evidence to identify growth and adjust routines; encourage peer observation for feedback.
column7: CASEL. (2020). SEL integrated instruction toolkit; Responsive Classroom. (n.d.). Morning meeting guide.
column8: When you taught _____, how did you explicitly model and practice the target social skill?

column5: Maintain weekly SEL mini-lessons and integrate social skill instruction into academic content; collaborate with grade-level team to share effective SEL strategies; invite students to co-create class norms and reflect on social skill growth.
column6: During observations, note how the teacher weaves SEL language into academic discussions; ask the teacher to share one successful SEL integration during PLCs; challenge them to mentor a colleague on SEL routines.
column7: Jones, S. et al. (2017). Social and emotional learning in schools: A guide to integrating with academics; Panorama Education. (n.d.). SEL activities and strategies.
column8: What impact did integrating SEL into your academic lesson have on student engagement?

column5: Script and rehearse one routine each week (entry, transitions, materials) with student input; use visual anchor charts to remind students of routine steps; collect data on transition times and aim to reduce them by 10–15%; invite a peer to observe and provide feedback.
column6: Model a routine rehearsal with the teacher, demonstrating clear expectations and positive reinforcement; provide transition timing tool; review data with the teacher and celebrate gains; encourage sharing of routine scripts at PLCs.
column7: Lemov, D. (2021). Teach Like a Champion 3.0—Technique 30: Tight Transitions; New Teachers Center. (n.d.). Effective classroom routines guide.
column8: How did practicing the new routine with students influence transition smoothness?

column5: Before each unit, send a concise overview to families outlining learning goals, essential vocabulary and success criteria; include questions parents can ask to support learning; ensure communications are translated and accessible; plan a personal introductory phone call or message to build rapport.
column6: Provide a template for unit overviews that includes learning targets and support tips; role-play a parent phone call with the teacher; review a draft communication for clarity and cultural responsiveness.
column7: Epstein, J. et al. (2019). School, Family, and Community Partnerships; Harvard Family Research Project. (2016). Tips for communicating student progress.
column8: How will you ensure families understand your unit’s expectations and can support their child’s learning?

column5: Develop a parent-friendly curriculum guide for the semester that explains units, key assignments, and rubrics; share exemplar student work samples with commentary to illustrate proficiency; host a virtual or in-person curriculum night to explain expectations and answer questions.
column6: Review the guide with the teacher and ensure it uses plain language and examples; co-plan the curriculum night agenda focusing on interactive discussion; gather parent feedback after the event for continuous improvement.
column7: National PTA. (2020). Standards for family-school partnerships; Great Schools Partnership. (2017). Communicating learning goals to families.
column8: What strategies will you use to make curriculum information engaging and understandable for families?
